**config_phase_path**: /Users/alireza/Downloads/repo/LLM4QBP/MAO/MAO(AiO version)/Code/CompanyConfig/Version-2.2/PhaseConfig.json

**task_prompt**: Module 2: Diagnostic Testing
This module describes recommended practices for the diagnostic testing for patients with CAP.
These recommendations apply in the ED, inpatient ward, or intensive care unit (ICU). The key
objectives of this module are to accurately and efficiently assess patients for CAP, so that
treatment can be initiated.

Module 2 Recommended Practices
Imaging for Diagnosis of CAP
• A chest x-ray is recommended for all patients presenting to the emergency department with
  symptoms of community-acquired pneumonia to confirm the diagnosis.
• A CT scan is not recommended.
• For patients without sepsis, wait for results of chest x-ray prior to giving antibiotics.

Diagnostic Tests
Patients admitted to hospital should receive the following tests (in addition to oxygen
saturation):
• Routine blood work: CBC, electrolytes, and renal function tests.
• If the patient meets >2 CRB-65 criteria, consider blood gases, lactate, liver enzyme and
  liver function tests.

Blood Culture
Blood cultures are recommended for patients who meet >2 SIRS criteria or require admission to
the ICU.
Note: SIRS criteria: Temperature: < 36°C or > 38°C; Heart rate: > 90 bpm; Respiratory Rate: >
20 breaths/min or PaCO2 < 32 mmHg; White Blood Cell Count: > 12,000 or < 4,000 cells/mm3 or >
10% bands.

Sputum Culture and Gram Stain
• Consider a sputum culture for patients admitted to hospital, if it is possible to obtain an
  adequate sample.
• If a sputum culture is obtained, consider Gram staining.
Note: If using a targeted antibiotic instead of using a non-targeted antibiotic, obtaining a
sputum culture is more important.

Urine Antigen Test
Urine antigen testing is recommended in the following instances:
• In high-severity patients.
• When there is an “enhanced surveillance directive” from Public Health Ontario on Legionella
  in inpatients.
• In patients who are not responding to drug therapy after 48–72 hours.
• For inpatients during peak season (mid-June to early October).
Field Evaluation: The panel recommends a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of
urine antigen testing in patients with CAP.

PCR for Legionella
Consider PCR testing for Legionella for intubated patients (e.g. high severity).

Abbreviations: ATS, American Thoracic Society; bpm, beats per minute; BTS, British Thoracic
Society; CAP, community-acquired pneumonia; CBC, complete blood count; CIDS, Canadian
Infectious Disease Society; CRB-65, assess confusion, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and
older than 65 years; CTS, Canadian Thoracic Society; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; ICU,
intensive care unit; IDSA, Infectious Disease Society of America; PCR, polymerase chain
reaction; NVALT, Dutch Association of Chest Physicians; PaCO2, partial pressure of carbon
dioxide; SIRS, systemic inflammatory response syndrome; SWAB, Dutch Working Party on Antibiotic
Policy.

Implementation Considerations
The following implementation considerations were expressed by members of the Expert Advisory
Panel concerning the module recommendations:
Urine Antigen Test
• Panel members suggested a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen
  testing would be useful to better understand its optimal use in the treatment of pneumonia.

Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Diagnostic Testing
This module describes recommended practices for the diagnostic testing for patients with CAP.
These recommendations apply in the ED, inpatient ward, or intensive care unit (ICU). The key
objectives of this module are to accurately and efficiently assess patients for CAP, so that
treatment can be initiated.

Module 2 Recommended Practices
Imaging for Diagnosis of CAP
• A chest x-ray is recommended for all patients presenting to the emergency department with
  symptoms of community-acquired pneumonia to confirm the diagnosis.
• A CT scan is not recommended.
• For patients without sepsis, wait for results of chest x-ray prior to giving antibiotics.

Diagnostic Tests
Patients admitted to hospital should receive the following tests (in addition to oxygen
saturation):
• Routine blood work: CBC, electrolytes, and renal function tests.
• If the patient meets >2 CRB-65 criteria, consider blood gases, lactate, liver enzyme and
  liver function tests.

Blood Culture
Blood cultures are recommended for patients who meet >2 SIRS criteria or require admission to
the ICU.
Note: SIRS criteria: Temperature: < 36°C or > 38°C; Heart rate: > 90 bpm; Respiratory Rate: >
20 breaths/min or PaCO2 < 32 mmHg; White Blood Cell Count: > 12,000 or < 4,000 cells/mm3 or >
10% bands.

Sputum Culture and Gram Stain
• Consider a sputum culture for patients admitted to hospital, if it is possible to obtain an
  adequate sample.
• If a sputum culture is obtained, consider Gram staining.
Note: If using a targeted antibiotic instead of using a non-targeted antibiotic, obtaining a
sputum culture is more important.

Urine Antigen Test
Urine antigen testing is recommended in the following instances:
• In high-severity patients.
• When there is an “enhanced surveillance directive” from Public Health Ontario on Legionella
  in inpatients.
• In patients who are not responding to drug therapy after 48–72 hours.
• For inpatients during peak season (mid-June to early October).
Field Evaluation: The panel recommends a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of
urine antigen testing in patients with CAP.

PCR for Legionella
Consider PCR testing for Legionella for intubated patients (e.g. high severity).

Abbreviations: ATS, American Thoracic Society; bpm, beats per minute; BTS, British Thoracic
Society; CAP, community-acquired pneumonia; CBC, complete blood count; CIDS, Canadian
Infectious Disease Society; CRB-65, assess confusion, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and
older than 65 years; CTS, Canadian Thoracic Society; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; ICU,
intensive care unit; IDSA, Infectious Disease Society of America; PCR, polymerase chain
reaction; NVALT, Dutch Association of Chest Physicians; PaCO2, partial pressure of carbon
dioxide; SIRS, systemic inflammatory response syndrome; SWAB, Dutch Working Party on Antibiotic
Policy.

Panel concerning the module recommendations:
Urine Antigen Test
• Panel members suggested a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen
  testing would be useful to better understand its optimal use in the treatment of pneumonia..

task: Module 2: Diagnostic Testing
This module describes recommended practices for the diagnostic testing for patients with CAP.
These recommendations apply in the ED, inpatient ward, or intensive care unit (ICU). The key
objectives of this module are to accurately and efficiently assess patients for CAP, so that
treatment can be initiated.

Module 2 Recommended Practices
Imaging for Diagnosis of CAP
• A chest x-ray is recommended for all patients presenting to the emergency department with
  symptoms of community-acquired pneumonia to confirm the diagnosis.
• A CT scan is not recommended.
• For patients without sepsis, wait for results of chest x-ray prior to giving antibiotics.

Diagnostic Tests
Patients admitted to hospital should receive the following tests (in addition to oxygen
saturation):
• Routine blood work: CBC, electrolytes, and renal function tests.
• If the patient meets >2 CRB-65 criteria, consider blood gases, lactate, liver enzyme and
  liver function tests.

Blood Culture
Blood cultures are recommended for patients who meet >2 SIRS criteria or require admission to
the ICU.
Note: SIRS criteria: Temperature: < 36°C or > 38°C; Heart rate: > 90 bpm; Respiratory Rate: >
20 breaths/min or PaCO2 < 32 mmHg; White Blood Cell Count: > 12,000 or < 4,000 cells/mm3 or >
10% bands.

Sputum Culture and Gram Stain
• Consider a sputum culture for patients admitted to hospital, if it is possible to obtain an
  adequate sample.
• If a sputum culture is obtained, consider Gram staining.
Note: If using a targeted antibiotic instead of using a non-targeted antibiotic, obtaining a
sputum culture is more important.

Urine Antigen Test
Urine antigen testing is recommended in the following instances:
• In high-severity patients.
• When there is an “enhanced surveillance directive” from Public Health Ontario on Legionella
  in inpatients.
• In patients who are not responding to drug therapy after 48–72 hours.
• For inpatients during peak season (mid-June to early October).
Field Evaluation: The panel recommends a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of
urine antigen testing in patients with CAP.

PCR for Legionella
Consider PCR testing for Legionella for intubated patients (e.g. high severity).

Abbreviations: ATS, American Thoracic Society; bpm, beats per minute; BTS, British Thoracic
Society; CAP, community-acquired pneumonia; CBC, complete blood count; CIDS, Canadian
Infectious Disease Society; CRB-65, assess confusion, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and
older than 65 years; CTS, Canadian Thoracic Society; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; ICU,
intensive care unit; IDSA, Infectious Disease Society of America; PCR, polymerase chain
reaction; NVALT, Dutch Association of Chest Physicians; PaCO2, partial pressure of carbon
dioxide; SIRS, systemic inflammatory response syndrome; SWAB, Dutch Working Party on Antibiotic
Policy.

Implementation Considerations
The following implementation considerations were expressed by members of the Expert Advisory
Panel concerning the module recommendations:
Urine Antigen Test
• Panel members suggested a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen
  testing would be useful to better understand its optimal use in the treatment of pneumonia.
Please design a process according to the task and output it in the following format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

Here are some examples of process text, and you can refer to these process texts but cannot copy them:
example1(Club Application Process):
<process>
  <activity role='students' action='fill out the Association application form' objects='' id='activity1'/>
  <activity role='club' action='Eligibility Checks' objects='Students club Application Form' id='activity2'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='Approved' id='branch1'>
      <activity role='' action='' objects='' id='activity3'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='Not approved' id='branch2'>
      <activity role='students' action='modify application form' objects='Student club Application Form' id='activity4'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <parallelGateway  id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch3'>
      <activity role='students' action='club interview' objects='' id='activity5'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch4'>
      <activity role='students' action='paying the interview fee' objects='interview fee' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <activity role='club' action='Rating' objects='Student interview results' id='activity7'/>
  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='score>=90' id='branch5'>
      <activity role='Club and students' action='club orientation party' objects='cakes,gifts' id='activity8'/>
      <activity role='students' action='participating in club activities' objects='' id='activity9'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='score>=80' id='branch6'>
      <activity role='students' action='get the outstanding student award' objects='prize' id='activity10'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='score<90' id='branch7'>
      <activity role='students' action='failure to join the club' objects='' id='activity11'/>
    </branch>
  </inclusiveGateway>
</process>
example2(Shipping process):
<process>
  <activity role='sender' action='Prepare to send a package' objects=' package' id='activity1'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='select pick-up' id='branch1'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
        <branch id='branch2'>
          <activity role='Sender' action='Select Courier pick-up location' objects='package' id='activity2'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch3'>
          <activity role='Express delivery company' action='Arrange a Courier' objects='' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      <activity role='Courier' action='receiving package' objects='package' id='activity4'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='choose to send by yourself' id='branch4'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
        <branch id='branch4'>
          <activity role='Sender' action='go to the mailing point to send' objects='package,mailing point address' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch5'>
          <activity role='Sender' action='Other ways' objects='package' id='activity6'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>
example3(Medical Process):
<process>
  <activity role='medical staff' action='Preliminary Check' objects='' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='medical staff' action='Assessment C' objects='' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='If X more than 10' id='branch2'>
          <activity role='medical staff' action='Procedure A' objects='' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='If X between 5 to 10' id='branch3'>
          <activity role='medical staff' action='Procedure B' objects='' id='activity4'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='Otherwise' id='branch4'>
          <activity role='medical staff' action='Skip' objects='' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch5'>
      <activity role='medical staff' action='Notify Relevant Team' objects='' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch6'>
      <activity role='medical staff' action='Monitor Variable Y at Regular Intervals' objects='' id='activity7'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch7'>
      <activity role='medical staff' action='Procedure D' objects='' id='activity8'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
        <branch condition='If not contradicted' id='branch8'>
          <activity role='medical staff' action='Relocate Entity' objects='' id='activity9'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='If contradicted' id='branch9'>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway_sub'>
            <branch condition='If condition G was satisfied' id='branch_sub1'>
              <activity role='medical staff' action='Perform option A' objects='' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='If condition H was satisfied' id='branch_sub2'>
              <activity role='medical staff' action='Perform option B' objects='' id='activity11'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch10'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='If No Prior Record' id='branch11'>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='Option Alpha' id='branch12'>
              <activity role='medical staff' action='Open new record using Alpha' objects='' id='activity12'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='Option Beta' id='branch13'>
              <activity role='medical staff' action='Open new record using Beta' objects='' id='activity13'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='If Prior Record Exists' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='medical staff' action='Skip' objects='' id='activity14'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
</process>
You must follow the bellow notes:
Note 1: You must include all tasks mentioned in the QBP's recommended practices section, regardless of perceived importance.
Note 2: Retain important information such as timeframe and frequency details within activity labels.
Note 3: As these are clinical pathways, use concurrency when there is no obvious sequence between activities.

[2026-07-04 19:37:41 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Team leader on : ProcessGeneration, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Diagnostic Testing
This module describes recommended practices for the diagnostic testing for patients with CAP.
These recommendations apply in the ED, inpatient ward, or intensive care unit (ICU). The key
objectives of this module are to accurately and efficiently assess patients for CAP, so that
treatment can be initiated.

Module 2 Recommended Practices
Imaging for Diagnosis of CAP
• A chest x-ray is recommended for all patients presenting to the emergency department with
  symptoms of community-acquired pneumonia to confirm the diagnosis.
• A CT scan is not recommended.
• For patients without sepsis, wait for results of chest x-ray prior to giving antibiotics.

Diagnostic Tests
Patients admitted to hospital should receive the following tests (in addition to oxygen
saturation):
• Routine blood work: CBC, electrolytes, and renal function tests.
• If the patient meets >2 CRB-65 criteria, consider blood gases, lactate, liver enzyme and
  liver function tests.

Blood Culture
Blood cultures are recommended for patients who meet >2 SIRS criteria or require admission to
the ICU.
Note: SIRS criteria: Temperature: < 36°C or > 38°C; Heart rate: > 90 bpm; Respiratory Rate: >
20 breaths/min or PaCO2 < 32 mmHg; White Blood Cell Count: > 12,000 or < 4,000 cells/mm3 or >
10% bands.

Sputum Culture and Gram Stain
• Consider a sputum culture for patients admitted to hospital, if it is possible to obtain an
  adequate sample.
• If a sputum culture is obtained, consider Gram staining.
Note: If using a targeted antibiotic instead of using a non-targeted antibiotic, obtaining a
sputum culture is more important.

Urine Antigen Test
Urine antigen testing is recommended in the following instances:
• In high-severity patients.
• When there is an “enhanced surveillance directive” from Public Health Ontario on Legionella
  in inpatients.
• In patients who are not responding to drug therapy after 48–72 hours.
• For inpatients during peak season (mid-June to early October).
Field Evaluation: The panel recommends a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of
urine antigen testing in patients with CAP.

PCR for Legionella
Consider PCR testing for Legionella for intubated patients (e.g. high severity).

Abbreviations: ATS, American Thoracic Society; bpm, beats per minute; BTS, British Thoracic
Society; CAP, community-acquired pneumonia; CBC, complete blood count; CIDS, Canadian
Infectious Disease Society; CRB-65, assess confusion, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and
older than 65 years; CTS, Canadian Thoracic Society; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; ICU,
intensive care unit; IDSA, Infectious Disease Society of America; PCR, polymerase chain
reaction; NVALT, Dutch Association of Chest Physicians; PaCO2, partial pressure of carbon
dioxide; SIRS, systemic inflammatory response syndrome; SWAB, Dutch Working Party on Antibiotic
Policy.

Implementation Considerations
The following implementation considerations were expressed by members of the Expert Advisory
Panel concerning the module recommendations:
Urine Antigen Test
• Panel members suggested a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen
  testing would be useful to better understand its optimal use in the treatment of pneumonia..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient meets 2 or fewer CRB-65 criteria' id='branch9'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='continue without CRB-65-triggered additional tests' objects='' id='activity9'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='patient meets more than 2 SIRS criteria or requires ICU admission' id='branch10'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='obtain blood cultures' objects='blood culture samples' id='activity10'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient meets 2 or fewer SIRS criteria and does not require ICU admission' id='branch11'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='do not routinely obtain blood cultures' objects='' id='activity11'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
        <branch condition='adequate sputum sample can be obtained' id='branch12'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='obtain sputum culture' objects='adequate sputum sample' id='activity12'/>
          <activity role='laboratory staff' action='consider Gram staining when sputum culture is obtained' objects='sputum specimen, Gram stain' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='adequate sputum sample cannot be obtained' id='branch13'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='document inability to obtain adequate sputum sample' objects='sputum sample availability' id='activity14'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <activity role='clinician' action='prioritize obtaining sputum culture when using a targeted antibiotic instead of a non-targeted antibiotic' objects='sputum culture, antibiotic strategy' id='activity15'/>
      <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='patient is high severity' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform urine antigen testing' objects='urine antigen test' id='activity16'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='there is an enhanced surveillance directive from Public Health Ontario on Legionella in inpatients' id='branch15'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform urine antigen testing' objects='urine antigen test' id='activity17'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient is not responding to drug therapy after 48-72 hours' id='branch16'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform urine antigen testing after 48-72 hours of non-response to drug therapy' objects='urine antigen test' id='activity18'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient is an inpatient during peak season from mid-June to early October' id='branch17'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform urine antigen testing during peak season from mid-June to early October' objects='urine antigen test' id='activity19'/>
        </branch>
      </inclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
        <branch condition='patient is intubated' id='branch18'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='consider PCR testing for Legionella' objects='Legionella PCR test' id='activity20'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient is not intubated' id='branch19'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='continue without intubation-triggered Legionella PCR consideration' objects='' id='activity21'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>

      <activity role='clinician' action='complete ED diagnostic testing pathway based on chest x-ray confirmation and oxygenation assessment' objects='chest x-ray results, oxygen saturation' id='activity22'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='clinical program team' action='conduct field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen testing in patients with CAP' objects='urine antigen testing field evaluation' id='activity23'/>
</process>

[2026-07-04 19:37:41 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Diagnostic Testing
This module describes recommended practices for the diagnostic testing for patients with CAP.
These recommendations apply in the ED, inpatient ward, or intensive care unit (ICU). The key
objectives of this module are to accurately and efficiently assess patients for CAP, so that
treatment can be initiated.

Module 2 Recommended Practices
Imaging for Diagnosis of CAP
• A chest x-ray is recommended for all patients presenting to the emergency department with
  symptoms of community-acquired pneumonia to confirm the diagnosis.
• A CT scan is not recommended.
• For patients without sepsis, wait for results of chest x-ray prior to giving antibiotics.

Diagnostic Tests
Patients admitted to hospital should receive the following tests (in addition to oxygen
saturation):
• Routine blood work: CBC, electrolytes, and renal function tests.
• If the patient meets >2 CRB-65 criteria, consider blood gases, lactate, liver enzyme and
  liver function tests.

Blood Culture
Blood cultures are recommended for patients who meet >2 SIRS criteria or require admission to
the ICU.
Note: SIRS criteria: Temperature: < 36°C or > 38°C; Heart rate: > 90 bpm; Respiratory Rate: >
20 breaths/min or PaCO2 < 32 mmHg; White Blood Cell Count: > 12,000 or < 4,000 cells/mm3 or >
10% bands.

Sputum Culture and Gram Stain
• Consider a sputum culture for patients admitted to hospital, if it is possible to obtain an
  adequate sample.
• If a sputum culture is obtained, consider Gram staining.
Note: If using a targeted antibiotic instead of using a non-targeted antibiotic, obtaining a
sputum culture is more important.

Urine Antigen Test
Urine antigen testing is recommended in the following instances:
• In high-severity patients.
• When there is an “enhanced surveillance directive” from Public Health Ontario on Legionella
  in inpatients.
• In patients who are not responding to drug therapy after 48–72 hours.
• For inpatients during peak season (mid-June to early October).
Field Evaluation: The panel recommends a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of
urine antigen testing in patients with CAP.

PCR for Legionella
Consider PCR testing for Legionella for intubated patients (e.g. high severity).

Abbreviations: ATS, American Thoracic Society; bpm, beats per minute; BTS, British Thoracic
Society; CAP, community-acquired pneumonia; CBC, complete blood count; CIDS, Canadian
Infectious Disease Society; CRB-65, assess confusion, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and
older than 65 years; CTS, Canadian Thoracic Society; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; ICU,
intensive care unit; IDSA, Infectious Disease Society of America; PCR, polymerase chain
reaction; NVALT, Dutch Association of Chest Physicians; PaCO2, partial pressure of carbon
dioxide; SIRS, systemic inflammatory response syndrome; SWAB, Dutch Working Party on Antibiotic
Policy.

Implementation Considerations
The following implementation considerations were expressed by members of the Expert Advisory
Panel concerning the module recommendations:
Urine Antigen Test
• Panel members suggested a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen
  testing would be useful to better understand its optimal use in the treatment of pneumonia..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Diagnostic Testing
This module describes recommended practices for the diagnostic testing for patients with CAP.
These recommendations apply in the ED, inpatient ward, or intensive care unit (ICU). The key
objectives of this module are to accurately and efficiently assess patients for CAP, so that
treatment can be initiated.

Module 2 Recommended Practices
Imaging for Diagnosis of CAP
• A chest x-ray is recommended for all patients presenting to the emergency department with
  symptoms of community-acquired pneumonia to confirm the diagnosis.
• A CT scan is not recommended.
• For patients without sepsis, wait for results of chest x-ray prior to giving antibiotics.

Diagnostic Tests
Patients admitted to hospital should receive the following tests (in addition to oxygen
saturation):
• Routine blood work: CBC, electrolytes, and renal function tests.
• If the patient meets >2 CRB-65 criteria, consider blood gases, lactate, liver enzyme and
  liver function tests.

Blood Culture
Blood cultures are recommended for patients who meet >2 SIRS criteria or require admission to
the ICU.
Note: SIRS criteria: Temperature: < 36°C or > 38°C; Heart rate: > 90 bpm; Respiratory Rate: >
20 breaths/min or PaCO2 < 32 mmHg; White Blood Cell Count: > 12,000 or < 4,000 cells/mm3 or >
10% bands.

Sputum Culture and Gram Stain
• Consider a sputum culture for patients admitted to hospital, if it is possible to obtain an
  adequate sample.
• If a sputum culture is obtained, consider Gram staining.
Note: If using a targeted antibiotic instead of using a non-targeted antibiotic, obtaining a
sputum culture is more important.

Urine Antigen Test
Urine antigen testing is recommended in the following instances:
• In high-severity patients.
• When there is an “enhanced surveillance directive” from Public Health Ontario on Legionella
  in inpatients.
• In patients who are not responding to drug therapy after 48–72 hours.
• For inpatients during peak season (mid-June to early October).
Field Evaluation: The panel recommends a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of
urine antigen testing in patients with CAP.

PCR for Legionella
Consider PCR testing for Legionella for intubated patients (e.g. high severity).

Abbreviations: ATS, American Thoracic Society; bpm, beats per minute; BTS, British Thoracic
Society; CAP, community-acquired pneumonia; CBC, complete blood count; CIDS, Canadian
Infectious Disease Society; CRB-65, assess confusion, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and
older than 65 years; CTS, Canadian Thoracic Society; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; ICU,
intensive care unit; IDSA, Infectious Disease Society of America; PCR, polymerase chain
reaction; NVALT, Dutch Association of Chest Physicians; PaCO2, partial pressure of carbon
dioxide; SIRS, systemic inflammatory response syndrome; SWAB, Dutch Working Party on Antibiotic
Policy.

Implementation Considerations
The following implementation considerations were expressed by members of the Expert Advisory
Panel concerning the module recommendations:
Urine Antigen Test
• Panel members suggested a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen
  testing would be useful to better understand its optimal use in the treatment of pneumonia.
process text:
<process>
  <activity role='clinician' action='assess patient presenting to the ED with symptoms of community-acquired pneumonia' objects='clinical presentation' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='clinician' action='order chest x-ray to confirm the diagnosis of CAP' objects='chest x-ray' id='activity2'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <activity role='clinician' action='avoid ordering CT scan because it is not recommended for CAP diagnosis' objects='CT scan' id='activity3'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='patient has sepsis' id='branch3'>
      <activity role='clinician' action='initiate antibiotics without waiting for chest x-ray results' objects='antibiotics' id='activity4'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient does not have sepsis' id='branch4'>
      <activity role='clinician' action='wait for chest x-ray results prior to giving antibiotics' objects='chest x-ray results, antibiotics' id='activity5'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='patient is admitted to hospital' id='branch5'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
        <branch id='branch6'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='measure oxygen saturation' objects='oxygen saturation' id='activity6'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch7'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform routine blood work' objects='CBC, electrolytes, renal function tests' id='activity7'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='patient meets more than 2 CRB-65 criteria' id='branch8'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='consider additional diagnostic tests' objects='blood gases, lactate, liver enzyme tests, liver function tests' id='activity8'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient meets 2 or fewer CRB-65 criteria' id='branch9'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='continue without CRB-65-triggered additional tests' objects='' id='activity9'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='patient meets more than 2 SIRS criteria or requires ICU admission' id='branch10'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='obtain blood cultures' objects='blood culture samples' id='activity10'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient meets 2 or fewer SIRS criteria and does not require ICU admission' id='branch11'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='do not routinely obtain blood cultures' objects='' id='activity11'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
        <branch condition='adequate sputum sample can be obtained' id='branch12'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='obtain sputum culture' objects='adequate sputum sample' id='activity12'/>
          <activity role='laboratory staff' action='consider Gram staining when sputum culture is obtained' objects='sputum specimen, Gram stain' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='adequate sputum sample cannot be obtained' id='branch13'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='document inability to obtain adequate sputum sample' objects='sputum sample availability' id='activity14'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <activity role='clinician' action='prioritize obtaining sputum culture when using a targeted antibiotic instead of a non-targeted antibiotic' objects='sputum culture, antibiotic strategy' id='activity15'/>
      <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='patient is high severity' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform urine antigen testing' objects='urine antigen test' id='activity16'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='there is an enhanced surveillance directive from Public Health Ontario on Legionella in inpatients' id='branch15'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform urine antigen testing' objects='urine antigen test' id='activity17'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient is not responding to drug therapy after 48-72 hours' id='branch16'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform urine antigen testing after 48-72 hours of non-response to drug therapy' objects='urine antigen test' id='activity18'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient is an inpatient during peak season from mid-June to early October' id='branch17'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform urine antigen testing during peak season from mid-June to early October' objects='urine antigen test' id='activity19'/>
        </branch>
      </inclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
        <branch condition='patient is intubated' id='branch18'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='consider PCR testing for Legionella' objects='Legionella PCR test' id='activity20'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient is not intubated' id='branch19'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='continue without intubation-triggered Legionella PCR consideration' objects='' id='activity21'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient is not admitted to hospital' id='branch20'>
      <activity role='clinician' action='complete ED diagnostic testing pathway based on chest x-ray confirmation and oxygenation assessment' objects='chest x-ray results, oxygen saturation' id='activity22'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='clinical program team' action='conduct field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen testing in patients with CAP' objects='urine antigen testing field evaluation' id='activity23'/>
</process>
Check which activities in the process text can be expanded into sub-activities and add these sub-activities to the process text. Please note:
1) Only expand important activities, not all of them.
2) Ensure that the process text remains logical when adding sub-activities.
3) Add appropriate gateways when adding activities.
4) When modifying the process text, note whether the newly added activity is a sequential, exclusive, or concurrent relationship.
5) Only expand activities if it's necessary and do not create overly detailed sub-activities.
Here are some examples of expanding activities in process texts into sub-activities. You can refer to them but cannot copy them:
1. 'payment with card/cash' activity with id='activity2' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Payment with card' and 'Payment with cash' 
   activity relations: exclusive
2. 'club interview id and confimation email' activity with id='activity5' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Interview at the club' and 'Send confirmation email'
   activity relations: sequential
3. 'Submit the materials' activity with id='activity9' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Submit interview materials' and 'Submit examination materials' 
   activity relations: parallel
After extending the activity, number the activity or gateway in addition to the original id. If the sub-activity are exclusive or parallel relationships, you need to add them in the form of gateways when modifying the process text:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

First output which activities can be extended according to the above format (...... can be expanded to:.......) and then give the modified process text. Don't say anything else.

**task_prompt**: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider

Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure Module 1: Risk Stratification Module 1 Recommended Practices 1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals. 1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis Initial investigations should include: • serum creatinine and electrolyte levels • troponin measurements • complete blood count • electrocardiogram • chest x-ray examination • an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available • frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized 1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing. High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing. High-risk markers include: • respiratory distress • hypoxemia • severity of pulmonary edema • poor response to furosemide administered in ED • hemodynamic compromise • significant arrhythmias • positive troponin 1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score). As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital. Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence. [2026-07-04 19:37:48 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider
Please design a process according to the task and output it in the following format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

Here are some examples of process text, and you can refer to these process texts but cannot copy them:
example1(Club Application Process):
<process>
  <activity role='students' action='fill out the Association application form' objects='' id='activity1'/>
  <activity role='club' action='Eligibility Checks' objects='Students club Application Form' id='activity2'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='Approved' id='branch1'>
      <activity role='' action='' objects='' id='activity3'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='Not approved' id='branch2'>
      <activity role='students' action='modify application form' objects='Student club Application Form' id='activity4'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <parallelGateway  id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch3'>
      <activity role='students' action='club interview' objects='' id='activity5'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch4'>
      <activity role='students' action='paying the interview fee' objects='interview fee' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <activity role='club' action='Rating' objects='Student interview results' id='activity7'/>
  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='score>=90' id='branch5'>
      <activity role='Club and students' action='club orientation party' objects='cakes,gifts' id='activity8'/>
      <activity role='students' action='participating in club activities' objects='' id='activity9'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='score>=80' id='branch6'>
      <activity role='students' action='get the outstanding student award' objects='prize' id='activity10'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='score<90' id='branch7'>
      <activity role='students' action='failure to join the club' objects='' id='activity11'/>
    </branch>
  </inclusiveGateway>
</process>
example2(Shipping process):
<process>
  <activity role='sender' action='Prepare to send a package' objects=' package' id='activity1'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='select pick-up' id='branch1'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
        <branch id='branch2'>
          <activity role='Sender' action='Select Courier pick-up location' objects='package' id='activity2'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch3'>
          <activity role='Express delivery company' action='Arrange a Courier' objects='' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      <activity role='Courier' action='receiving package' objects='package' id='activity4'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='choose to send by yourself' id='branch4'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
        <branch id='branch4'>
          <activity role='Sender' action='go to the mailing point to send' objects='package,mailing point address' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch5'>
          <activity role='Sender' action='Other ways' objects='package' id='activity6'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>
example3(Medical Process):
<process>
  <activity role='medical staff' action='Preliminary Check' objects='' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='medical staff' action='Assessment C' objects='' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='If X more than 10' id='branch2'>
          <activity role='medical staff' action='Procedure A' objects='' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='If X between 5 to 10' id='branch3'>
          <activity role='medical staff' action='Procedure B' objects='' id='activity4'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='Otherwise' id='branch4'>
          <activity role='medical staff' action='Skip' objects='' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch5'>
      <activity role='medical staff' action='Notify Relevant Team' objects='' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch6'>
      <activity role='medical staff' action='Monitor Variable Y at Regular Intervals' objects='' id='activity7'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch7'>
      <activity role='medical staff' action='Procedure D' objects='' id='activity8'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
        <branch condition='If not contradicted' id='branch8'>
          <activity role='medical staff' action='Relocate Entity' objects='' id='activity9'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='If contradicted' id='branch9'>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway_sub'>
            <branch condition='If condition G was satisfied' id='branch_sub1'>
              <activity role='medical staff' action='Perform option A' objects='' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='If condition H was satisfied' id='branch_sub2'>
              <activity role='medical staff' action='Perform option B' objects='' id='activity11'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch10'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='If No Prior Record' id='branch11'>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='Option Alpha' id='branch12'>
              <activity role='medical staff' action='Open new record using Alpha' objects='' id='activity12'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='Option Beta' id='branch13'>
              <activity role='medical staff' action='Open new record using Beta' objects='' id='activity13'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='If Prior Record Exists' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='medical staff' action='Skip' objects='' id='activity14'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
</process>
You must follow the bellow notes:
Note 1: You must include all tasks mentioned in the QBP's recommended practices section, regardless of perceived importance.
Note 2: Retain important information such as timeframe and frequency details within activity labels.
Note 3: As these are clinical pathways, use concurrency when there is no obvious sequence between activities.

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Diagnostic Testing
This module describes recommended practices for the diagnostic testing for patients with CAP.
These recommendations apply in the ED, inpatient ward, or intensive care unit (ICU). The key
objectives of this module are to accurately and efficiently assess patients for CAP, so that
treatment can be initiated.

Module 2 Recommended Practices
Imaging for Diagnosis of CAP
• A chest x-ray is recommended for all patients presenting to the emergency department with
  symptoms of community-acquired pneumonia to confirm the diagnosis.
• A CT scan is not recommended.
• For patients without sepsis, wait for results of chest x-ray prior to giving antibiotics.

Diagnostic Tests
Patients admitted to hospital should receive the following tests (in addition to oxygen
saturation):
• Routine blood work: CBC, electrolytes, and renal function tests.
• If the patient meets >2 CRB-65 criteria, consider blood gases, lactate, liver enzyme and
  liver function tests.

Blood Culture
Blood cultures are recommended for patients who meet >2 SIRS criteria or require admission to
the ICU.
Note: SIRS criteria: Temperature: < 36°C or > 38°C; Heart rate: > 90 bpm; Respiratory Rate: >
20 breaths/min or PaCO2 < 32 mmHg; White Blood Cell Count: > 12,000 or < 4,000 cells/mm3 or >
10% bands.

Sputum Culture and Gram Stain
• Consider a sputum culture for patients admitted to hospital, if it is possible to obtain an
  adequate sample.
• If a sputum culture is obtained, consider Gram staining.
Note: If using a targeted antibiotic instead of using a non-targeted antibiotic, obtaining a
sputum culture is more important.

Urine Antigen Test
Urine antigen testing is recommended in the following instances:
• In high-severity patients.
• When there is an “enhanced surveillance directive” from Public Health Ontario on Legionella
  in inpatients.
• In patients who are not responding to drug therapy after 48–72 hours.
• For inpatients during peak season (mid-June to early October).
Field Evaluation: The panel recommends a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of
urine antigen testing in patients with CAP.

PCR for Legionella
Consider PCR testing for Legionella for intubated patients (e.g. high severity).

Abbreviations: ATS, American Thoracic Society; bpm, beats per minute; BTS, British Thoracic
Society; CAP, community-acquired pneumonia; CBC, complete blood count; CIDS, Canadian
Infectious Disease Society; CRB-65, assess confusion, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and
older than 65 years; CTS, Canadian Thoracic Society; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; ICU,
intensive care unit; IDSA, Infectious Disease Society of America; PCR, polymerase chain
reaction; NVALT, Dutch Association of Chest Physicians; PaCO2, partial pressure of carbon
dioxide; SIRS, systemic inflammatory response syndrome; SWAB, Dutch Working Party on Antibiotic
Policy.

Implementation Considerations
The following implementation considerations were expressed by members of the Expert Advisory
Panel concerning the module recommendations:
Urine Antigen Test
• Panel members suggested a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen
  testing would be useful to better understand its optimal use in the treatment of pneumonia..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

activity7 can be expanded to:  
- sub-activity: 'perform complete blood count testing' and 'perform electrolyte testing' and 'perform renal function testing'  
- activity relations: parallel

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Diagnostic Testing This module describes recommended practices for the diagnostic testing for patients with CAP. These recommendations apply in the ED, inpatient ward, or intensive care unit (ICU). The key objectives of this module are to accurately and efficiently assess patients for CAP, so that treatment can be initiated. Module 2 Recommended Practices Imaging for Diagnosis of CAP • A chest x-ray is recommended for all patients presenting to the emergency department with   symptoms of community-acquired pneumonia to confirm the diagnosis. • A CT scan is not recommended. • For patients without sepsis, wait for results of chest x-ray prior to giving antibiotics. Diagnostic Tests Patients admitted to hospital should receive the following tests (in addition to oxygen saturation): • Routine blood work: CBC, electrolytes, and renal function tests. • If the patient meets &gt;2 CRB-65 criteria, consider blood gases, lactate, liver enzyme and   liver function tests. Blood Culture Blood cultures are recommended for patients who meet &gt;2 SIRS criteria or require admission to the ICU. Note: SIRS criteria: Temperature: &lt; 36°C or &gt; 38°C; Heart rate: &gt; 90 bpm; Respiratory Rate: &gt; 20 breaths/min or PaCO2 &lt; 32 mmHg; White Blood Cell Count: &gt; 12,000 or &lt; 4,000 cells/mm3 or &gt; 10% bands. Sputum Culture and Gram Stain • Consider a sputum culture for patients admitted to hospital, if it is possible to obtain an   adequate sample. • If a sputum culture is obtained, consider Gram staining. Note: If using a targeted antibiotic instead of using a non-targeted antibiotic, obtaining a sputum culture is more important. Urine Antigen Test Urine antigen testing is recommended in the following instances: • In high-severity patients. • When there is an “enhanced surveillance directive” from Public Health Ontario on Legionella   in inpatients. • In patients who are not responding to drug therapy after 48–72 hours. • For inpatients during peak season (mid-June to early October). Field Evaluation: The panel recommends a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen testing in patients with CAP. PCR for Legionella Consider PCR testing for Legionella for intubated patients (e.g. high severity). Abbreviations: ATS, American Thoracic Society; bpm, beats per minute; BTS, British Thoracic Society; CAP, community-acquired pneumonia; CBC, complete blood count; CIDS, Canadian Infectious Disease Society; CRB-65, assess confusion, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and older than 65 years; CTS, Canadian Thoracic Society; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; ICU, intensive care unit; IDSA, Infectious Disease Society of America; PCR, polymerase chain reaction; NVALT, Dutch Association of Chest Physicians; PaCO2, partial pressure of carbon dioxide; SIRS, systemic inflammatory response syndrome; SWAB, Dutch Working Party on Antibiotic Policy. Implementation Considerations The following implementation considerations were expressed by members of the Expert Advisory Panel concerning the module recommendations: Urine Antigen Test • Panel members suggested a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen   testing would be useful to better understand its optimal use in the treatment of pneumonia. |

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Diagnostic Testing This module describes recommended practices for the diagnostic testing for patients with CAP. These recommendations apply in the ED, inpatient ward, or intensive care unit (ICU). The key objectives of this module are to accurately and efficiently assess patients for CAP, so that treatment can be initiated. Module 2 Recommended Practices Imaging for Diagnosis of CAP • A chest x-ray is recommended for all patients presenting to the emergency department with   symptoms of community-acquired pneumonia to confirm the diagnosis. • A CT scan is not recommended. • For patients without sepsis, wait for results of chest x-ray prior to giving antibiotics. Diagnostic Tests Patients admitted to hospital should receive the following tests (in addition to oxygen saturation): • Routine blood work: CBC, electrolytes, and renal function tests. • If the patient meets &gt;2 CRB-65 criteria, consider blood gases, lactate, liver enzyme and   liver function tests. Blood Culture Blood cultures are recommended for patients who meet &gt;2 SIRS criteria or require admission to the ICU. Note: SIRS criteria: Temperature: &lt; 36°C or &gt; 38°C; Heart rate: &gt; 90 bpm; Respiratory Rate: &gt; 20 breaths/min or PaCO2 &lt; 32 mmHg; White Blood Cell Count: &gt; 12,000 or &lt; 4,000 cells/mm3 or &gt; 10% bands. Sputum Culture and Gram Stain • Consider a sputum culture for patients admitted to hospital, if it is possible to obtain an   adequate sample. • If a sputum culture is obtained, consider Gram staining. Note: If using a targeted antibiotic instead of using a non-targeted antibiotic, obtaining a sputum culture is more important. Urine Antigen Test Urine antigen testing is recommended in the following instances: • In high-severity patients. • When there is an “enhanced surveillance directive” from Public Health Ontario on Legionella   in inpatients. • In patients who are not responding to drug therapy after 48–72 hours. • For inpatients during peak season (mid-June to early October). Field Evaluation: The panel recommends a field evaluation to assess the clinical utility of urine antigen testing in patients with CAP. PCR for Legionella Consider PCR testing for Legionella for intubated patients (e.g. high severity).
